title: How to Read Financial Statements - Westmount Fundamentals
description: Revenue is vanity, profit is sanity, cash flow is reality. Learn to read all three financial statements and spot what companies try to hide.

@graph.headline: How to Read Financial Statements: A Beginner's Guide
@graph.description: Understand income statements, balance sheets, and cash flow — the foundation of investing. Learn how to read the three main financial statements.

mainEntity.name: Why is the cash flow statement often considered more important than the income statement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The income statement includes non-cash items (like depreciation) and recognizes revenue when earned, not when cash is received. The cash flow statement shows actual cash moving in and out, revealing a company's true ability to fund operations, pay debt, and return money to shareholders.

mainEntity.name: What is a good Current Ratio?

acceptedAnswer.text: A current ratio between 1.5 and 2.0 is generally considered healthy. Below 1.0 indicates potential short-term liquidity issues, while a very high ratio (e.g., above 3.0) might mean the company isn't using its assets efficiently.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between Gross Margin and Operating Margin?

acceptedAnswer.text: Gross margin only deducts the direct costs of making a product (COGS). Operating margin goes further by deducting all operating expenses like SG&A (Sales, General, and Administrative) and R&D, showing the profitability of core business operations.

mainEntity.name: Can a company be profitable but go bankrupt?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. If a company recognizes a lot of revenue on credit (boosting net income) but fails to collect cash, it may run out of money to pay its bills. This highlights the importance of the Operating Cash Flow metric.

mainEntity.name: Where can I find these financial statements for real companies?

acceptedAnswer.text: Publicly traded companies in the US file 10-K (annual) and 10-Q (quarterly) reports with the SEC. You can find these on the SEC EDGAR database, the company's investor relations website, or financial data sites like Yahoo Finance.
